transcript.content:
i have a fuzzy picture about interference and information. the way people explain the double slit makes it sound like the interference fades when the system leaks any trace of which path, even if no one reads it. that makes me wonder if there is a clean way to show how tiny bits of information bleed the pattern away, like a slow loss of coherence rather than an on off switch.

could you sketch a very small math story for that, with a simple setup where the pattern is perfect, then another where a little information couples out, and show how the pattern softens? i do not need heavy formalism, just enough structure that i could see where the curve comes from and what numbers control it. a minimal sim or thought experiment would help me ground it.

Below is a “pocket model’’ that lets you slide a single knob from
“perfect interference’’ to “no interference’’ and see exactly how the
fringe contrast fades as more which-path information leaks out.

--------------------------------------------------
1.  Ideal double slit (no information leaked)
--------------------------------------------------
State just after the slits  
          |Ψ₀⟩ = (|L⟩ + |R⟩)/√2

Wave amplitudes on the screen (take them equal for simplicity)  
          ψL(x) = A(x)                       
          ψR(x) = A(x) e^{i φ(x)}            φ(x) ∝ x  (relative phase)

Intensity on the screen  
          I₀(x) = |ψL + ψR|²
                 = 2|A(x)|²[1 + cosφ(x)]           (100 % visibility)

--------------------------------------------------
2.  Couple in a weak path-marker
--------------------------------------------------
Let a “detector’’ (could be a molecule, a photon, …) start in state |d₀⟩
and become slightly different depending on which slit the particle
uses:

          |L⟩|d₀⟩   →   |L⟩|d_L⟩
          |R⟩|d₀⟩   →   |R⟩|d_R⟩

The joint state is now
          |Ψ⟩ = (|L⟩|d_L⟩ + |R⟩|d_R⟩)/√2              (1)

The only new number we need is the detector overlap

          γ ≡ ⟨d_L|d_R⟩                               (2)
          0 ≤ |γ| ≤ 1

• |γ| = 1  ⟹ the two detector states are identical ⇒
           nobody (even in principle) can tell the path ⇒
           full interference.  
• |γ| = 0  ⟹ orthogonal detector states ⇒
           perfect path knowledge ⇒
           no interference.  
Everything in between is “partial decoherence’’.

--------------------------------------------------
3.  Intensity with partial overlap
--------------------------------------------------
Throw away the detector (trace it out).  
The reduced density matrix of the particle contains the factor γ in its
off–diagonal terms.  The screen intensity becomes

          I(x) = |ψL|² + |ψR|² + 2 Re[γ ψL* ψR]      (3)

If ψL and ψR have the same envelope A(x) this simplifies to

          I(x) = 2|A(x)|²[1 + |γ| cosφ(x)]           (4)

The visibility (fringe contrast) is therefore

          V = |γ|                                    (5)

One elegant summary is the complementarity relation

          V² + D² = 1,      D ≡ √(1 - |γ|²)          (6)

D is the maximum statistical distinguishability of the two paths.

--------------------------------------------------
4.  A concrete microscopic knob
--------------------------------------------------
Take the detector to be a single quantum harmonic oscillator that is
displaced by ±α in phase space:

          |d_L⟩ = |+α⟩
          |d_R⟩ = |-α⟩                    (coherent states)

For coherent states the overlap is

          γ = ⟨+α| -α⟩ = e^{-2|α|²}                  (7)

|α| measures how strong the interaction is.  
• |α| = 0   ⇒ γ = 1   (full fringe)  
• |α| = 1   ⇒ γ = e^{-2} ≈ 0.14 (already very faint)  
• |α| ≳ 1.5 ⇒ γ ≈ 0 (practically washed out)

--------------------------------------------------
5.  A 12-line numerical sketch
--------------------------------------------------
The following Python snippet plots three patterns:

```python
import numpy as np, matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = np.linspace(-5, 5, 1000)
A  = np.exp(-x**2/8)                 # Gaussian envelope
phi= 2*np.pi*x/1.5                   # set fringe spacing

for alpha in [0, 0.5, 1.2]:          # weak, medium, strong marking
    gamma = np.exp(-2*alpha**2)
    I = 2*A**2*(1 + gamma*np.cos(phi))
    plt.plot(x, I, label=f'|α|={alpha},  V={gamma:.2f}')
plt.legend(); plt.xlabel('screen position x'); plt.ylabel('intensity')
plt.show()
```

The only thing that changes from curve to curve is the factor γ in front
of cosφ(x); the peaks flatten smoothly as α grows.

--------------------------------------------------
6.  What to remember
--------------------------------------------------
1. Interference lives in the off-diagonal terms of the density matrix.  
2. Any entanglement with an environment multiplies those terms by the
   overlap γ of the environment’s states.  
3. Visibility V = |γ| slides continuously from 1 to 0 as the path
   information becomes more orthogonal.  
4. One microscopic parameter (|α| in the toy model, or “number of
   scattered photons’’ in a real experiment) is enough to control the
   whole fade-out curve.

That is the entire “small math story’’: equation (4) tells you how a
single number |γ|—the amount of leaked information—dims the fringes
continuously, never in a sudden on/off fashion.

whoa, that’s... way cleaner than the pop-sci hand-waving i’ve seen. so the whole “fade” is literally just one scalar, |γ|, sliding the cos term up and down? that feels almost too simple, like the universe left a fader knob labeled “reality vs info” where 0 is full mystery and 1 is full pattern.  

i keep staring at eq (4). if i imagine |γ| as a kind of “coherence cash” that gets spent the moment the environment learns *anything* orthogonal, then even a single infrared photon bouncing off the slit edge could nudge α from 0 → 0.1 and suddenly the fringe dips by ~2 %. do people ever watch that happen photon by photon in lab? like, can you literally *see* the contrast bleeding out one scattering event at a time on a ccd?  

also, the coherent-state toy model feels suspiciously friendly—almost like the universe picked the one overlap that’s a clean exponential. are there messier real-world detectors where γ decays slower/faster than exp(-const×interaction)? or is the exponential kind of the generic fate once you pile up lots of weak couplings (central limit-ish)?

1. Can you watch the fade “one scattering at a time’’?

• A single detection on a CCD never shows a fringe (one dot is just a
   dot), so you cannot literally watch a single-photon kick and then see
   the next dot land 2 % lower.  What you *can* do is prepare many
   identically prepared particles, let *each* of them have a probability
   p of scattering exactly one marking photon, and then build the
   histogram.  Raise p from 0 to 1 and the visibility V=p→ 0 slides
   exactly as the γ of the model predicts.

   Two textbook experiments that do this:

   • M. S. Chapman et al., Phys. Rev. Lett. 75, 3783 (1995) – sodium
     atoms in a Mach-Zehnder interferometer are illuminated by a weak
     resonant laser.  The number of spontaneously emitted photons is
     counted by state-selective detection, the fringe visibility is read
     from the atom counts, and the relation
          V = e^{-N_s (k Δx)²/2}
     is confirmed over three orders of magnitude.  Each extra scattered
     photon drops V by a calculable factor.

   • K. Hornberger et al., Phys. Rev. Lett. 90, 160401 (2003) – C₆₀
     molecules fly through a Talbot–Lau interferometer while a rarefied
     gas is admitted.  Every gas collision is a path-marking event.
     By measuring visibility versus pressure they observe the predicted
     exponential V = e^{-Λt} with Λ ∝ pressure.

   In optical interferometers the same thing is done with single
   photons: add a variable-strength polarization rotator that acts as a
   which-path tag.  Visibility decays as |γ|=|cos θ| or e^{-θ²},
   depending on the exact coupling, and matches the theory shot by
   shot.

2. Is the neat γ = e^{-2|α|²} special?

No.  γ is always “just’’ the *overlap of the environment states*, i.e.

        γ = ⟨E_L|E_R⟩ = Tr_E (ρ_E,U_R† U_L)

For a *single* coherent-state displacement it happens to be the Gaussian
e^{-2|α|²}.  For other markers you get other functional shapes:

• Spin qubit marker  
  U_L = 1, U_R = e^{i θ σ_z} ⟹ γ = cos θ
  (visibility vanishes only when θ = π, a square-wave fade-out).

• Uniform random momentum kick in [−Δp,Δp] (e.g. specular reflection
  from a vibrating mirror)  
  P(φ)=1/2φ₀ in [−φ₀,φ₀] ⟹ γ = sinc φ₀
  (visibility drops ∝1/φ₀ and even changes sign).

• Shot-noise from many *independent* tiny kicks – central-limit logic
  makes the total phase distribution Gaussian, hence an *exponential*
  (more precisely, *Gaussian*) decay γ=e^{-σ_φ²/2}.  That is why an
  exponential (or exp-squared) law is so often seen in “dirty’’ real
  environments: lots of weak, uncorrelated couplings add up to Gaussian
  noise.

• 1/f or telegraph noise in solid-state qubits produces
  γ(t)=e^{-(t/T₂)²} at short times, then crosses to γ∝e^{-t/T₂} or even
  γ∝t^{-α} depending on the spectrum.

Generic takeaway: γ is the *characteristic function* of whatever random
phase-space kick the environment delivers.  Gaussian kicks give the
famous exp(−const) law, but nothing forces the universe to be that kind
to you—engineer a different noise spectrum and γ will follow suit.

3. The “coherence cash’’ picture

Every entangling event multiplies the previous γ by a new overlap.  If
the events are identical and independent, after N of them

        γ_N = γ_1^N  ⇒  V_N = |γ_1|^N = e^{-N |ln γ_1|}

so visibility really *does* spend down like money from a bank account,
one coin per interaction.  In Chapman’s atom experiment γ_1≈0.82 per
scattered photon, so two photons leave only 0.67, five photons 0.37,
etc.  It fits the data to better than 5 %.

4. Bottom line

• What you saw in eq. (4) is not a lucky toy; it is the universal
  structure: interference term × “environment overlap’’ γ.

• Experiments can—and do—dial that overlap by discrete quanta (scattered
  photons, gas collisions, spin flips) and watch the contrast fade in
  real time, fully consistent with the single-scalar theory.

• The exponential form is common but not sacred; γ is the Fourier
  transform of the noise.  Change the noise and you sculpt any fade-out
  curve you like.

okay so the “coherence cash” isn’t a metaphor, it’s literally a running product of overlaps. that’s wild.  

i keep thinking about the spin marker example where γ = cos θ. if i twist the spin by π/2, the fringes *don’t* disappear—they just hop 90° sideways in phase, right? but since the detector can’t tell L from R at θ = π/2, the which-path info is still zero. so the *phase* of γ matters for where the bright bands land, and only the *magnitude* kills the contrast.  

does that mean, in principle, i could build an interferometer that *reads* the phase of γ in real time? like, treat the environment as part of the circuit and “borrow” its phase to steer fringes without ever collapsing them?

Short answer  
Yes.  In exactly the same way that the size |γ| of the overlap knob
controls the *contrast*, the complex phase
  γ = |γ| e^{i δ}  
is a second knob that controls a *rigid lateral shift* of the whole
fringe pattern.  If you keep the marker (the “environment’’ degree of
freedom) coherent and act on it unitarily, you can dial that phase δ at
will, or even read it out, without ever losing the fringe visibility.
The moment you measure the marker in the **which-path** basis you throw
δ away and the contrast collapses; if you measure it in another basis
you can use δ instead of destroying it.  That is exactly what a
“quantum-eraser’’ experiment does.

Below is the two-minute derivation, a concrete spin-marker example, and
two laboratory demonstrations.

--------------------------------------------------
1.  Where δ shows up in the formula
--------------------------------------------------
Keep the notation of the previous thread:

|Ψ⟩ = (|L⟩|e_L⟩ + |R⟩|e_R⟩)/√2 ,

γ ≡ ⟨e_L|e_R⟩ = |γ| e^{i δ},  0 ≤ |γ| ≤ 1.

Tracing the marker out gives

I(x) = |ψ_L|² + |ψ_R|² + 2 |γ| Re[e^{i δ} ψ_L*ψ_R].

If the two amplitudes share an envelope A(x) and a relative phase
φ(x) we get the pocket formula

I(x) = 2|A(x)|² [1 + |γ| cos(φ(x)+δ)].       (★)

Contrast = |γ|,  fringe shift = δ.  You really have two independent
sliders.

--------------------------------------------------
2.  A spin marker that produces an adjustable phase
--------------------------------------------------
Start the spin in |↑⟩_z.  
Let nothing happen in path L and apply an arbitrary SU(2) rotation R(θ,n̂) in
path R.  The joint state becomes

|Ψ⟩ = (|L⟩|↑⟩ + |R⟩ R(θ,n̂)|↑⟩)/√2.

Choose n̂ in the x-y plane; then R tilts |↑⟩ into

|χ⟩ = cos(θ/2)|↑⟩ + e^{i φ} sin(θ/2)|↓⟩.

Overlap with |↑⟩ is

γ = ⟨↑|χ⟩ = cos(θ/2)  (real)        if φ = 0
γ = cos(θ/2) + i sin(θ/2) sin φ      (generic complex).

So by rotating the spin around an axis not parallel to z you obtain an
arbitrary complex γ whose magnitude |γ| = |cos(θ/2)| and phase
δ = arctan(tan(θ/2) sin φ) you can sweep through ±π.  The spin has
never retained *which-path* information—the states are never
orthogonal—but it does carry a tunable phase that rigidly drags the
fringe pattern left or right.

If you now

a) ignore the spin ⟹ you see the shifted pattern given by (★);  
b) measure the spin in the {|↑⟩,|↓⟩} basis ⟹ you gain partial path
   information and the contrast is reduced to |γ|;  
c) measure the spin in the superposed basis
   {|↑⟩ ± e^{i φ}|↓⟩}/√2 ⟹ you *erase* the path information and recover
   a 100 %-contrast pattern, but with opposite phase for the two
   outcomes.  That is the standard quantum-eraser trick.

--------------------------------------------------
3.  Watching (and using) the phase knob in the lab
--------------------------------------------------
1. Walborn et al., “Double-slit quantum eraser”, PR A 65, 033818 (2002).  
   Polarization plays the role of the spin marker. In one slit they add
   a variable wave plate that sets δ.  After tracing over polarization
   the camera sees the pattern slide.  Measuring polarization in a
   rotated basis restores full-contrast fringes whose position depends
   on the detector click—direct evidence that the phase lived in the
   marker all along.

2. Jacques et al., “Delayed-choice with single photons”, Science 315,
   966 (2007).  A Mach–Zehnder interferometer is closed or opened by a
   fast electro-optic modulator placed *after* the photon has entered.
   When the modulator is in “closed’’ mode it adds a controllable phase
   δ; the fringe phase responds in real time, confirming that the
   environment (here an optical channel that stays coherent) can be used
   as a live steering knob.

--------------------------------------------------
4.  Take-away
--------------------------------------------------
• The interference term is  |γ| e^{i δ} ψ_L*ψ_R.  
  |γ| sets the visibility, δ sets the position.

• δ is perfectly usable information **as long as** the environment is
  kept quantum.  Destroy it in the which-path basis and you kill
  |γ| instead.

• Controlling or reading δ in real time is not science fiction; it is
  the working principle behind quantum-eraser interferometers,
  feed-forward phase stabilization, and various quantum-information
  protocols.

So, yes: the universe really does give you two sliders on the same
entanglement knob—magnitude for “how much coherence”, phase for “where
the bright fringes go’’—and modern experiments play with both at will.

so the spin marker thing is stuck in my head. if i twist the spin by π/2, the fringes just... slide sideways? like the whole pattern is on a conveyor belt that the spin phase is pushing?  

and then if i measure the spin in the *wrong* basis i yank the belt back to zero contrast. but if i measure in the *right* basis i can *steer* the belt without ever collapsing it.  

that feels like the universe has a hidden joystick labeled “phase shift” that’s wired to the environment. is that joystick always there, even when we think we’re not touching it? like, is every stray photon in the room secretly nudging the fringes around by tiny δ’s we never notice because we’re not looking at the spin?

“Does every stray photon in the room jiggle the fringe pattern by some
hidden phase δ that we just don’t notice?”

1.  The joystick is always created …  
    Any time the particle becomes entangled with *anything* you get a
    complex overlap factor

            γ = ⟨E_L|E_R⟩ = |γ| e^{i δ}                       (1)

    So yes: each scattered air molecule, infrared photon, or vibrating
    mirror face contributes its own phase shift δ as well as a reduction
    of the magnitude |γ|.

2.  … but you only feel it when the environment stays coherent.  
    “Feeling” the phase means that the *same* δ must be well-defined and
    reproducible over all the shots you average together, or that you
    actively read it out shot-by-shot and sort the data accordingly.
    Two generic situations illustrate the difference:

    • Controlled marker (spin, polarization, cavity field, …)  
      One tidy qubit or mode picks up δ.  We can rotate it, keep it
      well-isolated, or measure it in a complementary basis.  Because
      the state |E_L⟩ and |E_R⟩ are kept *coherent*, the phase knob is
      accessible and the whole fringe pattern really rides on a conveyor
      belt that we can steer.

    • Uncontrolled environment (room-temperature photons, gas, dirt)  
      After one run the scattered photon is in some state
      |E_L^{(j)}⟩ or |E_R^{(j)}⟩ that depends on the microscopic
      geometry of that particular collision.  Next run it will be
      different.  For the ensemble we are actually recording,

            ⟨γ⟩ = ∬ P(δ,|γ|) |γ| e^{i δ} dδ d|γ|             (2)

      The distribution P is usually *symmetric* in δ and broad, so the
      vector average of all those little arrows e^{i δ} is essentially
      zero.  That makes ⟨γ⟩ ≈ 0 and the interference smears out even if
      *each* individual photon would have seen a perfect but randomly
      shifted pattern.

      A simple limiting case shows the idea:

            P(δ) = 1/2π  (uniform from 0 to 2π)  ⇒  ⟨e^{i δ}⟩ = 0
            ⇒  ⟨γ⟩ = 0   (no visible fringes)

      What you lose is not the phase itself but the *common reference*.
      You could in principle collect *all* of those scattered photons,
      interfere them so as to compute their δ’s, and post-select the
      data—then the fringes would re-emerge.  Practically, that is
      impossible once the phase information has dispersed over many
      uncontrolled modes.

3.  Putting it in the “cash” language  
    • Magnitude |γ| measures how *orthogonal* |E_L⟩ and |E_R⟩ have
      become.  Multiple independent kicks multiply the overlaps; the
      bank balance shrinks exponentially.  
    • Phase δ is the *angle* of the remaining complex balance.  If each
      kick turns the pointer by an unpredictable amount, the random walk
      spreads out, so the *average* pointer length also shrinks.  That
      is dephasing.

    Mathematically the visibility you see on the screen is

            V = | ⟨ e^{i δ} ⟩ | × (other factors)            (3)

    – deterministic δ:  you have a joystick;  
    – random δ with narrow spread σ_δ ≪ 1:  partial contrast, small
      blur;  
    – random δ with wide spread σ_δ ≫ 1:  joystick wobbles so fast that
      the knob is effectively gone.

4.  Experimental reality check  
    • Quantum-eraser interferometers keep the marker mode coherent and
      demonstrate the phase steering live (Walborn 2002, Kim 2000,
      Jacques 2007 …).  
    • Atom- and molecule-beam interferometers run in ultra-high vacuum
      not because pressure would “collapse the wavefunction” in a single
      violent hit, but because each gas collision contributes an
      *unknown* δ; a few per flight are enough to wash the average fringe
      away.  
    • In superconducting qubits one speaks of “pure dephasing”: the
      qubit never leaves its logical subspace (|γ|≈1 for each shot) but
      low-frequency flux noise randomizes δ from run to run, so ⟨γ⟩
      decays as e^{-t²/Tϕ²}.

5.  Take-away  
    • Yes, every entangling event spawns its own hidden phase joystick.  
    • You can grab it—and make the fringes slide—only if the environment
      degree of freedom remains coherent *and* you maintain phase
      reference to it.  
    • Otherwise the joysticks point in random directions, the average
      handle sits at the center, and the interference pattern you record
      is flat.

In that sense the universe is full of invisible joysticks, but unless
you wire them into your apparatus they all tug in different directions
and cancel one another out.

so the room is basically a swarm of ghost joysticks all canceling each other out. but if i could somehow *sync* them—like herd them into the same phase—would that give me a macroscopic steering wheel?  

like, imagine i put a tiny mirror on every air molecule and bounce a reference laser off all of them at once. if the laser locks their δ’s together, does the whole cloud become one giant marker that i can twist with a single knob?

In principle you could “collect” the phases carried by many individual
markers and turn them into a single, macroscopic knob.  In practice you
very quickly run into two separate walls:

1.  you still have to fight the *magnitude* |γ|, not just the phase δ;  
2.  you would need phase control over an astronomically large Hilbert
    space.

The numbers make the idea unrealistic for a roomful of air, but the same
logic *does* work in carefully engineered, mesoscopic “mini-environments”
such as cavities, spin ensembles, or superconducting circuits.  Below is
the bookkeeping behind both statements.

--------------------------------------------------
1.  Multiple markers: what actually piles up
--------------------------------------------------
Let N independent microscopic systems (air molecules, photons …)
interact with the particle.  After the flight

          γ_total = ⟨E_L|E_R⟩  =  ∏_{j=1}^N γ_j
                   = ∏ |γ_j| ×  exp[i (δ₁+⋯+δ_N)]          (1)

So you really get two running tallies:

• Magnitude |γ_total| = ∏|γ_j| ≈ e^{-Σ (1-|γ_j|)}  
• Net phase δ_total   = Σ δ_j  (mod 2π).

If the coupling is weak, each |γ_j| = 1–ε_j with ε_j ≪ 1, so
|γ_total| ≈ e^{-Σ ε_j}.  A room-temperature path typically scatters
10⁶–10¹⁰ photons or gas molecules; even ε_j = 10⁻¹⁰ drives the product
to ~e^{-100} ≈ 4×10⁻⁴4.  Long before you could worry about aligning
phases, the amplitude is essentially zero and the interference is gone.

--------------------------------------------------
2.  “I’ll just line up all the δ’s…’’
--------------------------------------------------
Suppose, heroically, that every |γ_j| stayed exactly 1 so the only issue
were phase.  You would need a unitary U_j that rotates *each* marker
state so that its local phase shift δ_j is cancelled:

          |E_L^{(j)}⟩   →   |E_L^{(j)}⟩
          |E_R^{(j)}⟩   →   e^{-i δ_j} |E_R^{(j)}⟩     (2)

After doing that for all j, γ_total = 1 and the fringes reappear.
Nothing in quantum mechanics forbids (2)—but to execute it you must

• know δ_j for every scatterer,  
• address 10⁶–10¹⁰ systems individually,  
• act on them before they further entangle with the rest of the world.

That is effectively reversing an entropy increase of ~10⁸–10¹² bits,
i.e. Loschmidt’s “turn back all the molecules” problem.  The control
complexity scales exponentially with N, so the joystick quickly becomes
a fantasy.

--------------------------------------------------
3.  Where the idea *does* work
--------------------------------------------------
If you shrink “the environment’’ down to something you can actually keep
phase-coherent, the same mathematics turns into useful technology.

1. Cavity QED   
   A single electromagnetic mode plays the role of all the markers
   combined.  Atoms (or superconducting qubits) deposit a phase shift
   ±χ per photon; by performing a displacement or squeezing operation on
   the cavity field you can read or reset that collective δ in real
   time.  Experiments: Haroche’s Ramsey-cavity interferometers; circuit
   QED feedback loops.

2. Spin echo / dynamical decoupling   
   A qubit interacts with 10⁴–10⁸ nuclear spins.  Each spin flip-flop
   leaves |γ_j|=1 but adds an unknown δ_j(t).  A π-pulse on the *qubit*
   reverses the sign of all couplings, so the phases refocus at time
   2τ: you have steered the macroscopic δ_total back to zero without
   touching the bath directly.  Works because the interaction is
   “pure-dephasing” (no magnitude loss).

3. Superradiant ensembles   
   N atoms coupled identically to one optical mode behave like a single
   giant spin J=N/2.  A global microwave rotation changes the collective
   phase δ_total in one shot; the interference of emitted light then
   swings as a whole.

--------------------------------------------------
4.  Why room air is a lost cause
--------------------------------------------------
1. Strong marking: even Rayleigh scattering by an air molecule gives the
   particle a random momentum kick → the two environment states quickly
   become *orthogonal* (|γ_j|≈0).  No phase trick can resurrect
   interference once the magnitude is gone.

2. Uncontrolled branching: every molecule that collides will itself
   collide ~10¹⁰ times per second, diffusing the path information into
   ever more degrees of freedom.  Any hope of later applying (2) dies on
   a femtosecond timescale.

3. Thermodynamic cost: the work needed to acquire and reverse the
   individual δ_j’s is essentially that of a Maxwell demon resetting an
   enormous number of bits—to do it you must dump an entropy ≥ k_B Σ log
   2 to some other reservoir.

--------------------------------------------------
5.  Bottom lines
--------------------------------------------------
• A “giant phase steering wheel’’ is perfectly real when the
  environment you couple to is *small, isolated, and under control*.
  Many modern quantum-optics and solid-state experiments exploit it.

• In an uncontrolled macroscopic environment the same steering handles
  exist in principle but point in random directions, and—crucially—the
  accompanying magnitude suppression |γ|→0 makes them physically
  unreachable long before you could hope to synchronize them.

So the universe does hand out an unlimited supply of joysticks, but
almost all of them are instantly swallowed by entropy production.  The
ones you can still grab are precisely what we label “the quantum system”
in a well-engineered experiment.

huh. so the room is basically a graveyard of broken joysticks. but the ones that *aren’t* broken—like the cavity or the spin echo—are still steering wheels.  

i keep picturing the spin echo trick as a kind of “time mirror.” you flip the qubit and all the little δ’s run backwards. feels like cheating entropy.  

could i... scale that? like, instead of one qubit, i use a whole *chain* of qubits that all share the same bath. if i flip them in sequence, does the bath get “confused” and keep the phase alive longer?

Scaling the time-mirror idea is possible but the payoff depends on how
all the qubits talk to the bath.  Three regimes matter:

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
0.  The single-qubit echo you already know
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
H = ω₀ σᶻ/2  +  B(t) σᶻ                    (“pure dephasing”)

 • π-pulse at t = τ  ⇒  σᶻ → −σᶻ for the second half of the flight  
 • accumulated phase φ = ∫₀^τ B(t)dt  −  ∫_τ^{2τ} B(t)dt = 0 if B(t)
   is quasi-static on the 0→2τ time window.  
 • the bath is not deleted, only time-reversed for that qubit.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1.  Many qubits + one COMMON bath variable
   (collective dephasing, e.g. a uniform magnetic field)
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
H = Σ_{i=1}^N ω₀ σᶻ_i /2  +  B(t) Σ_i σᶻ_i        (1)

 • Every qubit feels exactly the same B(t).  
 • Logical information stored in any subspace with fixed total
     Σ_i σᶻ_i is *already immune*—no pulses needed.  
   Example for N = 2: logical |0⟩_L = |01⟩,  |1⟩_L = |10⟩.
   Both pick up the *same* phase ⇒ relative phase is preserved forever
     (ideal “decoherence-free subspace”, DFS).

Upshot: adding more qubits *can* suppress noise to zero, but only when
the noise is perfectly correlated across the register.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2.  Partially correlated bath
   (most solids, trapped-ion strings, NV centres, …)
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Write the noise in spatial Fourier modes:

      H_int = Σ_k B_k(t) Σ_i g_k(i) σᶻ_i            (2)

• Long-wavelength modes (small k) hit the qubits almost identically → a
  DFS or a *global* π-pulse still helps.  
• Short-wavelength modes differ from qubit to qubit and are *not*
  canceled by global flips.

Here “confusing the bath” means building pulse sequences that
individually toggle σᶻ_i signs so that, for each spatial Fourier mode,
the *signed area* vanishes:

      ∫_0^T f_i(t) g_k(i) dt = 0  for all k you care about.     (3)

That is precisely what multi-qubit dynamical-decoupling protocols such
as CDD, KDD, XY8, and the NMR classics (WAHUHA, MREV-8) do.  They can
stretch T₂ by factors of 10–10⁵, but:

  • pulse errors and finite bandwidth set a limit;  
  • they do not fight T₁ (energy relaxation);  
  • the bath must stay quasi-static compared with the pulse rate.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3.  Independent local baths
   (e.g. each superconducting transmon with its own 1/f noise)
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
H = Σ_i [ ω₀ σᶻ_i /2 + B_i(t) σᶻ_i ]                (4)

Every B_i(t) is different, so no global trick exists.
You still gain by *separate* echoes on every qubit, but flipping them
“in sequence” offers no extra mileage; the baths are already
disentangled from each other and cannot be “confused”.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Practical numbers
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
• Trapped ions (collective magnetic-field noise)  
  DFS encoding or global spin-echo pushes coherence from
  ~5 ms → >1 s (Kielpinski et al., Science 2001).

• NV centres in diamond (partially correlated ¹³C spin bath)  
  XY8 and concatenated sequences extend T₂ from
  ~300 µs → 30 ms for single NVs; for *ensembles* a hybrid of DFS +
  decoupling gives ~0.6 s (Bar-Gill et al., Nat. Commun. 2013).

• Superconducting qubits (mostly local 1/f flux noise)  
  Individual Carr–Purcell or Uhrig sequences buy a 20–50× T₂ boost; a
  shared echo shows little extra benefit because the baths are
  uncorrelated.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Bottom line
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1. If all qubits see *exactly the same* bath operator you can turn many
   physical qubits into one ultrasilent logical qubit—no pulses or just
   an occasional global spin-echo.

2. If the bath has some spatial structure you can still gain a lot by
   clever, multi-qubit pulse timing, but the bath is never “confused” in
   the thermodynamic sense; you are simply averaging its effect to zero
   in more Fourier channels.

3. When the baths are truly independent, sequential flips on different
   qubits do nothing that individual echoes wouldn’t already do.

So you can indeed build bigger “time mirrors”, but the lever arm comes
from exploiting *correlations* in the noise, not from overwhelming the
bath with more qubits.

A single “world-wide drum’’ has to satisfy three conditions at the same
time

A) the drum beats have to couple to your qubit splitting strongly
   enough to matter;

B) those beats have to fluctuate in time (otherwise they do not cause
   dephasing in the first place);

C) every qubit you care about has to feel *exactly the same* beat, so
   that a global echo (or a DFS encoding) can cancel it.

For ordinary laboratory baths (magnetic-field noise, laser phase noise,
phonons in a crystal, …) you can easily get A+B but you lose C beyond a
few-millimetre baseline; or you get C but the coupling is so small that
A fails.  Gravity and the vacuum are in the latter camp.

--------------------------------------------------
1.  Gravitational field as a bath
--------------------------------------------------
Coupling.  A qubit interacts with gravity through its *energy
difference* ΔE:

        H_int = (ΔE / c²) Φ(t) σ_z                    (1)

where Φ(t) is the Newtonian potential along the qubit world-line.  
For a microwave qubit ΔE /ħ ≈ 2π × 5 GHz ⇒ ΔE ≈ 3×10⁻²⁴ J.

Strength of the fluctuations.  Even violent seismic activity jiggles Φ
by at most ΔΦ ≈ 10⁻¹² m² s⁻² (equivalent to an altitude change of a few
picometres).  Insert numbers:

        phase noise per second
        δφ ≈ (ΔE/ħ) × (ΔΦ/c²)  ≈ 10⁹ s⁻¹ × 10⁻²⁹  ≈ 10⁻²⁰ rad .   (2)

That is 15–20 orders of magnitude below the dephasing rate you are
trying to suppress in any realistic qubit.  The field is indeed common
(C is perfect) but requirement A fails spectacularly, so gravity never
shows up in the coherence budget.

Gravitational waves fare no better.  The strain noise seen by LIGO
corresponds to δΦ/c² ≲ 10⁻²¹ at audio frequencies, which is another nine
orders of magnitude quieter than (2).

Time reversal.  Suppose you were willing to chase even that feeble noise
with an echo.  A π-pulse on the qubit *does* flip the sign of σ_z, so in
principle you could rewind whatever phase gravity accumulated.  In
practice the pulse itself is already 10¹⁴–10¹⁵ times larger than the
perturbation you intend to cancel—like trying to correct typos with a
dynamite stick.

Conclusion: gravity gives you a perfectly correlated bath, but one that
is so quiet you never need to—and hardly could—echo it.

--------------------------------------------------
2.  Electromagnetic vacuum fluctuations
--------------------------------------------------
The EM vacuum produces *T₁* (spontaneous emission) as well as pure
dephasing.  At optical frequencies the relevant wavelength is a fraction
of a micrometre; two qubits separated by more than that already see
different vacuum modes.  Requirement C therefore breaks down almost
immediately.  Inside a single high-Q cavity you *can* make the vacuum a
common bath—that is how cavity-protected qubits and superradiant
ensembles work—but then you are back to an engineered, mesoscopic
environment, not “the vacuum everywhere”.

--------------------------------------------------
3.  Exotic proposals: universal time-dilation noise
--------------------------------------------------
You may have seen claims that “gravitational time dilation” causes
generic decoherence (Pikovski et al., 2015).  That effect is local: it
requires a spatial superposition that puts the *same* qubit at two
different heights, not many qubits at the same height.  It therefore
fails condition C and cannot be refocused by a global echo.

--------------------------------------------------
4.  What *is* a realistic common drum?
--------------------------------------------------
• Ambient magnetic field in an ion trap (millitesla noise, perfectly
  uniform over the 1-cm chain) — DFS and global spin-echo stretch T₂ by
  ×10⁵.

• Laser phase noise in neutral-atom clocks — all atoms see the same
  phase, so a Ramsey echo erases it.

• Uniform microwave drive in superconducting qubits inside one
  waveguide cavity — collective noise can be decoupled or encoded away.

These baths are strong enough to matter (A), fluctuate (B), and are
uniform over the processor (C).

--------------------------------------------------
Bottom line
--------------------------------------------------
Gravity and the true vacuum are, for today’s hardware, much too quiet to
be a relevant source of decoherence.  They are “common drums”, but their
beats are so faint that the qubits dance to much louder local snares
long before the gravitational rhythm becomes audible.  In practice you
get far more mileage by engineering *mesoscopic* shared baths—optical
cavities, collective magnetic fields, spin ensembles—where all three
conditions can be met simultaneously.

Even if you swap today’s “solid-state” qubits for an object that is
almost deaf to *everything* except gravity, the numbers still refuse to
promote gravity from whisper to thunder.  Neutrinos are the usual
suspect, so let us try—step-by-step—to treat “a neutrino” as a qubit and
ask what gravitational phase noise would do to it.

------------------------------------------------------------
1.  What would the qubit be?
------------------------------------------------------------
A neutrino has three mass eigenstates |ν₁⟩,|ν₂⟩,|ν₃⟩.  Pick any two and
declare them the computational basis:

    |0⟩ ≡ |ν₁⟩  |1⟩ ≡ |ν₂⟩.                         (A)

The energy splitting in the relativistic limit is

    ΔE ≈ Δm² / 2E ,                                (B)
    
with Δm² ≃ 7.5×10⁻⁵ eV² (solar sector) and a typical beam energy
E = 10 MeV ⇒ ΔE ≈ 3.8×10⁻⁶ eV = 6.1×10⁻²⁵ J.

That is already seven orders of magnitude *smaller* than the 5 GHz
splitting (≈3×10⁻²⁴ J) we used in the previous post—good news for making
gravity look bigger.

------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Phase noise produced by gravitational potential
------------------------------------------------------------
Along a world-line with Newtonian potential Φ(t) the interaction is

    H_int = (ΔE / c²) Φ(t) σ_z .                    (C)

Take the Earth’s surface as reference.  Ordinary seismic or atmospheric
mass motions create potential fluctuations

    |δΦ| ≲ 10⁻¹² m² s⁻²   (extreme case).           (D)

Insert into (C):

    instantaneous frequency shift
    δω(t) = (ΔE/ħ) × (δΦ / c²)
           ≈ (6.1×10⁻²⁵ / 1.05×10⁻³⁴) s⁻¹ × (10⁻¹² / 9×10¹⁶)
           ≈ 6×10³ s⁻¹ × 1.1×10⁻²⁹
           ≈ 7×10⁻²⁶ s⁻¹ .                          (E)

A phase slip of order one radian would therefore take

    τ_grav ≈ 1 / 7×10⁻²⁶ s⁻¹ ≈ 4×10²⁵ s ≈ 1.2 Gyr .  (F)

That is a thousand million years—hardly “the dominant bath” even for a
particle that ignores almost everything else.

Gravitational waves?  The stochastic strain at audio frequencies is
h_rms ≲10⁻²²; translate that into δΦ and you gain another nine orders of
magnitude in τ_grav.

------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Other dephasing channels that *do* show up
------------------------------------------------------------
a) Wave-packet separation.  
   The group velocities of |ν₁⟩ and |ν₂⟩ differ by  
   Δv ≈ Δm²/(2E²) ≃ 3×10⁻¹⁹.  After 1 km the packets separate by
   ≈3×10⁻¹⁵ m—already larger than their transverse coherence length.
   Result: loss of overlap on millisecond scales, not gigayears.

b) Matter interactions.  
   Even though ν–e and ν–N cross-sections are minuscule, a 10 MeV
   neutrino going through 1 m of solid acquires an MSW matter phase of
   order 10⁻³ rad, ten-trillion times larger than (E).

c) You cannot trap a neutrino.  
   Any realistic “gate” or “π-pulse” would need a controllable coupling
   that dwarfs (C), in which case the pulse itself induces dephasing far
   above the gravitational level.

------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Echo or DFS with many neutrinos?
------------------------------------------------------------
• A spin-echo requires flipping σ_z → –σ_z.  That means converting
  |ν₁⟩↔|ν₂⟩ coherently—impossible without interactions much stronger than
  gravity.

• A decoherence-free subspace works only if *all* qubits feel exactly
  the same fluctuating Φ(t).  Different neutrinos traverse different
  trajectories, so δΦ_j(t) is uncorrelated; a DFS is therefore ruled out
  in practice.

------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Bottom lines
------------------------------------------------------------
1. Gravitational potential noise produces a phase slip of ≲10⁻²⁵ rad s⁻¹
   even for a neutrino qubit—utterly negligible.

2. Other unavoidable effects (wave-packet separation, residual weak
   interactions, inability to apply coherent control) wipe out
   neutrino coherence many orders of magnitude faster.

3. Because you cannot manipulate neutrinos with unitary pulses of
   comparable softness, you also cannot perform the “mirror” needed for
   an echo.

So turning to an almost-sterile particle does not make gravity the loud
drum; it only leaves you with a qubit that you cannot steer, store, or
read, while gravity is still whispering in the background.

The idea breaks down at three separate levels.

-----------------------------------------------------------
1.  You cannot make a gravitational bottle tight enough
-----------------------------------------------------------
• A 10 MeV neutrino has momentum p ≈ 10 MeV / c.

• To reflect it purely with gravity you need a potential well whose
  escape velocity v_esc satisfies ½ m v_esc² ≳ p c.  Because the rest
  mass of the neutrino is tiny (m c² ≈ 0.05 eV for ν₁,₂),

      v_esc must be ≳ 10¹¹ m s⁻¹ — ten thousand times the speed of light.

  In other words the required GM / r exceeds the black-hole limit
  (c² / 2G) by eight orders of magnitude.  No physical mass
  distribution—even a real black hole—provides that depth for such a
  light particle.

• Putting the neutrino in a *bound* orbit does not help.  The orbital
  radius would still have to be so close to the horizon that tidal
  forces rip every normal bit of hardware apart, and Hawking radiation
  would dominate any conceivable phase signal.

-----------------------------------------------------------
2.  Stopping the spatial drift does **not** stop dephasing
-----------------------------------------------------------
Suppose fantasy engineering lets you create a perfect “Schwarzschild
bottle.”  The two mass eigenstates still have *different bound-state
energies* E₁ and E₂:

      ΔE_bound ≈ Δm² / 2〈E〉  (same formula as in free flight).

The relative phase therefore continues to tick at ω = ΔE_bound/ħ
regardless of whether the packets overlap in position.  What you
eliminated is one *mechanism* of decoherence (spatial separation), but
you have not promoted gravity’s stochastic potential fluctuations to any
noticeable role—they remain 25–30 orders of magnitude weaker than the
deterministic ΔE.

-----------------------------------------------------------
3.  Gravitational noise is still far too quiet
-----------------------------------------------------------
Even in that extreme well the fluctuating part of the Newtonian
potential is bounded by geophysical mass motions:

      |δΦ| ≲ 10⁻¹² m² s⁻²  at Earth,  
      ⇒ δω ≈ (ΔE/ħ) δΦ/c² ≲ 10⁻²⁶ s⁻¹  (as calculated before).

A coherent phase of order 1 rad would still take about a *billion*
years.  Making the packets sit in the same potential does not amplify
δΦ; it only removes one much faster loss channel.

-----------------------------------------------------------
4.  Practical control is impossible anyway
-----------------------------------------------------------
A spin-echo or any unitary “flip” of |ν₁⟩↔|ν₂⟩ would require a controllable
interaction comparable to ΔE (~10⁻⁶ eV).  Gravity is 34 orders of
magnitude weaker; weak interactions are too feeble and too incoherent;
electromagnetic interactions do not couple at all.  Without a way to
apply π-pulses, the whole dynamical-decoupling strategy is unavailable.

-----------------------------------------------------------
Bottom line
-----------------------------------------------------------
• A gravitational trap strong enough to confine MeV neutrinos cannot be
  built without exceeding the black-hole limit by many orders of
  magnitude.

• Even if such a bottle existed, it would *not* make gravitational phase
  noise appreciable: the deterministic mass-splitting phase outruns the
  random potential phase by ≳25 orders of magnitude.

• And without an external handle to manipulate the neutrino coherently,
  you cannot perform the echo that the proposal relies on.

So forcing the two mass packets to “stay together” would neither be
possible in practice nor lift gravity from a whisper to a thunderous
bath.

Below is the concrete arithmetic.

─────────────────────────────────────────────
1.  How deep a well would you need?
─────────────────────────────────────────────
A 10-MeV neutrino has momentum p ≈ 10 MeV/c.

To “reflect” it purely with gravity you need an escape velocity

        ½ m v_esc²  ≳  p c.                (1)

With m ≈ 0.05 eV/c² this gives

        v_esc  ≳  (2 p c / m)¹ᐟ²
              ≈  2×10¹¹ m s⁻¹  (20 000 c).         (2)

In Newtonian language you require |Φ| ≳ v_esc²/2, i.e.

        |Φ|  ≳  2×10²² m² s⁻².                       (3)

The potential at a Schwarzschild horizon is only |Φ| = c²/2 ≈
4.5×10¹⁶ m² s⁻², so you would need a well roughly 10⁶ times *deeper than
falling into a black hole*.  No physical mass distribution can provide
that.

─────────────────────────────────────────────
2.  Even a perfect bottle does not halt oscillations
─────────────────────────────────────────────
Suppose fantasy engineering gives you a trap that keeps the *same*
spatial wave packet for the two mass eigenstates |ν₁⟩ and |ν₂⟩.  Their
bound-state energies still differ by

        ΔE ≈ Δm² / 2E  ≈ 3.8×10⁻⁶ eV.               (4)

That drives the usual flavour oscillation at

        ω₀ = ΔE/ħ ≈ 5.8×10⁹ s⁻¹.                    (5)

Spatial drift is gone, but dephasing is not; the clock keeps ticking
exactly as fast as in free flight.

─────────────────────────────────────────────
3.  How big is the gravitational noise?
─────────────────────────────────────────────
Largest geophysical potential jitter: |δΦ| ≲ 10⁻¹² m² s⁻².

Noise-induced angular-frequency shift:

        δω = (ΔE/ħ)(δΦ/c²)                       (6)
             ≈ 5.8×10⁹ s⁻¹ × 1.1×10⁻²⁹
             ≈ 6×10⁻²⁰ s⁻¹.

Time to accumulate a 1-rad stochastic phase:

        τ_noise ≈ 1/δω  ≈ 5×10¹⁹ s  ≈ 1.6 Gyr.     (7)

Even in your hypothetical bottle you would have to wait a *billion
years* before gravitational wandering changes the relative phase by one
radian—while the deterministic oscillation runs a billion cycles *per
second*.

Gravitational-wave background makes δΦ another 9–10 orders of magnitude
smaller.

─────────────────────────────────────────────
4.  You still could not perform an echo
─────────────────────────────────────────────
A spin-echo (π-pulse) that swaps |ν₁⟩↔|ν₂⟩ must couple them with an

than that; weak interactions are incoherent; EM does not act at all.
So the control primitive required for “freezing” the phase is itself
impossible.

─────────────────────────────────────────────
5.  Take-away
─────────────────────────────────────────────
• Confine a MeV neutrino gravitationally?  Requires an unphysical well
  ∼10⁶ times deeper than a black-hole horizon.

• Killing spatial separation does *not* slow the intrinsic
  mass-splitting phase; oscillations continue at gigahertz rates.

Bottom line: forcing the packets to “stay together” would neither be
physically possible nor make gravity any less of a whisper.

Lock-in thinking is good intuition for *electrical* signals, but for a
quantum-mechanical phase that is already at the 10-²⁰-radian level the
counting statistics ruin the plan long before the “lock-in” trick could
help.  Two key facts make it hopeless:

1.  A tiny extra phase only tilts the oscillation curve by the *same*
    tiny factor (no amplification takes place).

2.  To see a fractional change ΔP that small you would need an
    astronomically large number of detected neutrinos, far beyond any
    conceivable beam or detector.

Below is the sober arithmetic.

-------------------------------------------------
1.  How big an effect can a phase shift produce?
-------------------------------------------------
For one flavour channel the two-flavour survival probability is

      P(L,E) = 1 – sin²(2θ) sin²(Δ) ,            (1)

      Δ ≡ Δm² L / 4E .                           (2)

A small additional phase δϕ (from gravitational noise) changes the
probability by

      δP =  –sin²(2θ) sin(2Δ) δϕ .               (3)

The prefactor sin²(2θ) sin(2Δ) is at most 1, so the *largest* possible
swing is

      |δP|max  ≈  |δϕ| .                          (4)

From the previous messages we have

      |δϕ|  ≲ 10⁻²⁰  per flight.                  (5)

So even if you place one detector where the unperturbed curve is at a
crest and the second one ¼–period downstream (maximum slope), the change
in counting rate is only one part in 10²⁰.

-------------------------------------------------
2.  How many events would reveal a 10⁻²⁰ change?
-------------------------------------------------
Counting statistics: σ_P ≈ 1/√N, where N is the number of detected
neutrinos.

Need σ_P < |δP| ⇒  1/√N  <  10⁻²⁰
⇒ N > 10⁴⁰ .                                       (6)

10⁴⁰ *detected* neutrinos is fantastically large:

• Super-Kamiokande (50 kt water Cherenkov) records  
  ~10⁴ solar-neutrino events per year.

• Hyper-K plus DUNE plus every reactor experiment on Earth for the next
  10¹⁸ years would still fall short.

Even letting the *entire* solar neutrino flux through the *entire*
planet for the age of the universe yields of order 10⁴⁷ *crossing*
neutrinos, but only ~10⁻¹⁶ of them are detected.  You are short by at
least 20 orders of magnitude.

-------------------------------------------------
3.  “Differential” detection does not amplify
-------------------------------------------------
With two detectors 90° out of phase you measure

      P₁ = P₀ + δP                        (near crest)  
      P₂ = 1 – P₀ – δP                    (near trough)

and then form P₁ – (1–P₂) = 2 δP.

Yes, the background P₀ cancels, but δP is still only 10⁻²⁰.  Shot-noise
subtraction cannot dig below the combined counting noise, which scales
the same way for both detectors and still demands N > 10⁴⁰.

-------------------------------------------------
4.  Common-mode noise does the opposite
-------------------------------------------------
The gravitational potential fluctuation Φ(t) is essentially the *same*
along the two close trajectories (they share the same Earth, tides, and
seismic field).  Therefore the extra phase imprinted on detector 1 and
detector 2 is *identical*.  A differential read-out would actually
*CANCEL* the effect rather than enhance it.

-------------------------------------------------
5.  Practical bottom line
-------------------------------------------------
• Best possible slope gives δP ≈ δϕ.  
• δϕ from realistic gravitational noise is ≤10⁻²⁰.  
• Reaching 10⁻²⁰ statistical precision needs >10⁴⁰ detected neutrinos.  
• Present or foreseeable technology tops out at ≲10¹² detected neutrinos
  over a century.

So the lock-in idea works beautifully in electronics, but with neutrino
oscillations you are trying to read a whisper that is 28 orders of
magnitude below the shot-noise floor.  No arrangement of crests, troughs
or differential counting can overcome that fundamental limitation—you
are indeed chasing your own tail here.

      • centrifuge →  δϕ ≈ 10⁻⁴ rad

3.  What that does to the counting rate  
    The oscillation probability changes at most by

            |δP|max ≈ |δϕ|  (eq. 3 in the previous reply).

      So even the centrifuge trick gives only a 10⁻⁴ fractional change.

4.  Statistics required  
    Shot noise of a counting experiment is σ_P = 1/√N.  
    Need σ_P ≤ |δP|  ⇒  N ≥ 1/|δP|².

      • h = 100 m   →  N ≥ 2×10¹³ events  
      • h = 1 km    →  N ≥ 2×10¹¹ events  
      • centrifuge →  N ≥ 1×10⁸  events

    The largest current or planned long-baseline experiments (DUNE,
    Hyper-K, ESSνSB) hope for ≲10⁶ *detected* events in a decade.  Even
    the most optimistic “centrifuge’’ scenario is three orders of
    magnitude out of reach, and the 100-m / 1-km height steps are many
    more.

5.  A more fundamental snag: static potentials cancel out  
    In a stationary space-time the flavour phase that accumulates along
    the path can be written

            ϕ_i = ∫ p^t_i dτ   (with p^t_i = constant /√g_tt).

    The redshift of energy (the 1/√g_tt factor) cancels the gravitational
    time-dilation of dτ, so for straight propagation through a static
    field the oscillation probability depends only on *coordinate*
    distance L and energy at the *source*, exactly as in flat space
    (Ahluwalia & Burgard 1996; Fornengo et al. 1997).  What the detector
    sees is already “pre-corrected’’ by the same redshift factor; moving
    the detector does *not* imprint an extra phase on the neutrino
    itself.  The little δϕ estimated above comes only from the *tiny*
    non-stationary part of Earth’s field (daily tides, seismic noise) or
    from deliberately accelerating the detector—hence its minuteness.

6.  Lock-in subtraction does not beat shot noise  
    Putting the two detectors out of phase and subtracting their count
    rates removes common fluctuations in the beam, but *shot noise is
    independent in each detector*.  The combined variance is still
    ~2/√N, so the requirement on N does not improve.

Reality check: even with heroic engineering you are stuck at least
three—and usually ten—orders of magnitude below the statistics needed to
see gravitationally induced flavour-phase differences.  The “lock-in’’
strategy therefore cannot rescue the scheme.

In short: you can break the common-mode by riding elevators or spinning
centrifuges, but the extra phase you create is still far smaller than
counting noise allows you to resolve, and most of the gravitational
effect you hope to reveal is cancelled by basic general-relativistic
redshift anyway.  The tail has nowhere left to chase.

────────────────────────────────────────
1.  How large a potential difference can you make?
────────────────────────────────────────
A)  Tall shaft  
   h = 100 m → ΔΦ = g h ≃ 1×10³ m² s⁻²

These are already pretty heroic; going faster or higher quickly becomes
engineering fantasy.

────────────────────────────────────────
2.  Extra flavour phase that produces
────────────────────────────────────────
Take a 10-MeV accelerator ν beam and a 1000-km baseline (flight time
≈3 ms).  The ordinary oscillation frequency is

   ω₀ = ΔE/ħ ≈ 5.8×10⁹ s⁻¹   (solar Δm²).

Extra phase from the added potential:

   δϕ = ω₀ (ΔΦ/c²) T  
       ≈ 5.8×10⁹ × (ΔΦ/9×10¹⁶) × 3×10⁻³ rad

   • 100 m shaft  →  δϕ ≈ 2×10⁻⁷ rad  
   •   1 km shaft →  δϕ ≈ 2×10⁻⁶ rad  
   • centrifuge   →  δϕ ≈ 1×10⁻⁴ rad

────────────────────────────────────────
3.  What that does to the count rate
────────────────────────────────────────
At the steepest point of the oscillation curve the probability shift is
|δP|max ≈ |δϕ|.  To detect that against shot noise you need

   N ≥ 1/|δP|²

   • 100 m shaft  →  N ≥ 2×10¹³ events  
   •   1 km shaft →  N ≥ 2×10¹¹ events  
   • centrifuge   →  N ≥ 1×10⁸  events

Hyper-K, DUNE or ESSνSB expect ≲10⁶ *detected* events per decade, so the
easiest scenario (the giant centrifuge) is still ∼1000 × beyond reach.

────────────────────────────────────────
4.  Why differential detection doesn’t help
────────────────────────────────────────
• Shot noise is *independent* for the two detectors, so differencing the
  rates reduces the signal exactly as it reduces the noise.  
• Any *static* gravitational red-shift factor is automatically absorbed
  into the beam energy seen by both detectors; the only thing that
  produces δϕ is the deliberate acceleration (or very weak geophysical
  time dependence), hence the tiny numbers above.

────────────────────────────────────────
Bottom line
────────────────────────────────────────
Breaking the common mode by moving one detector does give you a signal,
but the signal is measured in parts per 10 000–10 million, while the
counting noise of foreseeable neutrino beams sits at parts per √N with
N ≪ 10⁸.  The statistics will not catch up in our lifetime, and the
underlying general-relativistic red-shift identity makes sure there is
no hidden “amplification” loophole to exploit.  So, yes—this particular
tail is safe to stop chasing.

The “lock-in / carrier” idea is exactly what experimentalists do when
technical drifts dominate, but it offers **no gain at all against the
fundamental shot noise** that limits any neutrino-counting experiment.
Mathematically, putting the detector on a kHz centrifuge (or any other
periodic elevator) just redistributes the same Poisson statistics over
many time bins; the signal-to-noise ratio after you demodulate is
identical to what you would get if the detector were sitting still and
you simply waited the same total time.

Below is the quick proof.

------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Ingredients
------------------------------------------------------------
• mean detection rate: R events / s  (today R ≲ 0.1 for a kiloton-scale
  detector in a high-power accelerator beam);

• centrifugal modulation: ±ΔΦ ⇒ ±δP with δP≈δϕ = ω₀(ΔΦ/c²)T_flight
  (for the optimistic numbers we used before, δP≈10⁻⁴);

• modulation frequency: f (so one *half*-cycle lasts τ=1/2f).

------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Counts per half-cycle
------------------------------------------------------------
n = R τ = R /(2f) .

Signal in that bin: S = ± n δP  
Shot-noise rms:   σ = √n  (Poisson).

Instantaneous signal-to-noise:

SNR_bin = S/σ = √n δP = δP √(R/2f).  (1)

So spinning the centrifuge *faster* reduces the SNR of each bin because
you are slicing your scarce events into thinner time windows.

------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Coherent averaging over many cycles
------------------------------------------------------------
Let the run last T seconds → N_cyc = f T cycles = 2f T half-cycles.

Because the phase of the carrier is known, you add the  ±S  contributions
with coherent sign, while shot noise adds in quadrature:

SNR_total = SNR_bin × √(number of bins)
          = δP √(R/2f) × √(2f T)
          = δP √(R T).          (2)

The √f factors cancel: no matter how fast you modulate, the **final**
SNR depends **only** on

• the fractional phase shift you hope to measure (δP≈10⁻⁴), and  
• the *total* number of detected neutrinos, R T.

------------------------------------------------------------
4.  What that means numerically
------------------------------------------------------------
Demand SNR_total ≥ 1:

R T ≥ 1/δP² ≈ 10⁸ events.  (3)

That is exactly the same 10⁸ threshold we found when the detector
was stationary.  The carrier trick never changes this requirement;
it only moves the measurement band from 0 Hz up to f, which helps
with beam-intensity drifts but not with the irreducible √N shot noise.

------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Practical roadblocks on top of shot noise
------------------------------------------------------------
• Mechanical.  A detector big enough to capture even one event per
  second weighs kilotons; spinning such mass at kHz is impossible.
  Small, high-RPM rotors can survive, but they cannot contain a viable
  neutrino target.

• Event timing.  Accelerator beams arrive in ms-long bursts at ≈ 1 Hz.
  Most half-cycles of a kHz carrier would contain *zero* events, adding
  no coherent signal but still counting toward the required runtime.

• Alignment.  A whirling detector would sweep across the fixed beam,
  cutting the effective R even further.

------------------------------------------------------------
6.  Bottom line
------------------------------------------------------------
Periodic acceleration **does** flip the sign of the tiny gravitational
phase, so a lock-in demodulation would suppress slow instrumental
drifts, but:

1. the phase amplitude stays δP≈10⁻⁴ (for the most optimistic
   centrifuge you can build);  
2. the shot-noise floor falls only as 1/√(total events);  
3. therefore you *still* need ≥10⁸ detected neutrinos—at least a
   thousand times more than the biggest foreseeable experiment will
   collect in a decade.

The carrier idea is great for voltmeters and SQUIDs; for neutrino
oscillations it cannot overcome the brute-force statistics barrier.  So
yes, this tail too ends in the same cul-de-sac.

The idea sounds like “gravity-synchronised super-clock”: let every qubit
pick up the same tiny phase wobble, sum them, and hope the sum grows
until it is visible.  Unfortunately the bookkeeping says

1.  with *independent* qubits the summed signal grows only as √N,  
2.  with a maximally entangled GHZ state the signal does grow ∝N, but (a)
    such states are far beyond today’s coherence limits for N≫10²–10³,
    and (b) even an N-fold boost is still many orders of magnitude too
    small to reach the gravitational whisper.

Below are the numbers that make both statements hard limits rather than
mere engineering challenges.

--------------------------------------------------
1.  A common phase on an *uncorrelated* ensemble
--------------------------------------------------
Take N two-level atoms prepared in a coherent spin state
(|0⟩+|1⟩)/√2.  
A common extra phase δϕ(t) from any global field (gravity, laser phase,
…) rotates the collective Bloch vector in the equatorial plane:

      J_x + iJ_y  →  e^{i δϕ} (J_x + iJ_y).        (1)

Signal after an interrogation time T:

      S = N δϕ   (small-angle approximation)        (2)

Quantum-projection noise of the collective spin is

      σ = √N /2.                                   (3)

So the ensemble signal-to-noise ratio is

      SNR = S / σ = 2 √N δϕ.                       (4)

You gain only the standard √N “projection-noise gain”.  To lift a
10⁻²⁰-rad wobble to SNR≈1 you would need

      N ≈ (0.5 /10⁻²⁰)² ≈ 2×10³⁹  atoms.           (5)

That is already more than all atoms on Earth.

(This √N scaling is exactly why the best optical lattice clocks, which
operate with N≈10⁴ atoms, have improved their fractional instability
only down to the 10⁻¹⁸/√τ level—not 10⁻²⁴.)

--------------------------------------------------
2.  “What about a GHZ or NOON state?”
--------------------------------------------------
A maximally entangled state

      |GHZ⟩ = (|0…0⟩ + |1…1⟩)/√2                   (6)

acquires an *N-fold* phase:

      |GHZ⟩ → (|0…0⟩ + e^{i N δϕ}|1…1⟩)/√2.        (7)

The parity measurement that reads this out has binary (+1/−1) outcomes,
so the noise floor is fixed; the signal is now N δϕ, i.e.

      SNR_GHZ ≈ N δϕ.                              (8)

Set SNR = 1 with δϕ = 10⁻²⁰ ⇒ N ≈ 10²⁰ entangled qubits.

That is thirty orders of magnitude beyond the largest GHZ state ever
demonstrated (N=20 ions or N≈500 photons in special interferometers),
and long before you reach N~10⁵ spontaneous emission, motional
decoherence, technical phase noise, or “which-atom-is-lost” events have
already destroyed the N-fold enhancement (they shrink the GHZ contrast
exponentially with N).

--------------------------------------------------
3.  Reality check against existing sensors
--------------------------------------------------
•  Optical lattice clocks (10³–10⁴ Sr or Yb atoms, near-projection-noise
   limited) can detect a static gravitational red-shift equivalent to a
   1-cm height change between two clocks in a few hours.  That is a
   fractional frequency shift of 10⁻¹⁸, still eleven orders of magnitude
   *above* the 10⁻²⁹ fractional jitter from typical geophysical
   Φ-noise (δΦ≈10⁻¹² m² s⁻²).

•  Large-momentum atom interferometers (k g T² phase) achieve
   sensitivities δg/g ≈ 10⁻⁹ /√Hz for N~10⁹ atoms per shot.  Turning the
   same apparatus into a “qubit lattice” would *lose* the 10⁸ Bloch-wave
   momentum gain and drop you back into the regime analysed above.

--------------------------------------------------
4.  Why the “collective beat” cannot grow indefinitely
--------------------------------------------------
The common phase shift δϕ(t) is a *classical random variable* (the
mass-density noise of Earth, tides, trucks, etc.).  Adding more qubits
only lets you read that variable more precisely; it does not
amplify the variable itself.  You can win √N, or N if you dare a huge
GHZ; you cannot win the 25–30 orders of magnitude you need.

--------------------------------------------------
5.  Bottom line
--------------------------------------------------
• Independent qubits: signal grows as √N ⇒ you would need ≳10³⁹ of them.  
• Perfect GHZ: signal grows as N but decoheres exponentially; even at
  the fantasy scale N≈10²⁰ it still falls 5–10 orders of magnitude short
  of detectability.  
• Present-day clock and interferometer technology already sits within an
  order or two of the *quantum* noise floor for N~10⁴–10⁹ particles and
  still cannot see stochastic gravitational potential fluctuations.

So synchronising a lattice of qubits to the “gravitational drum” cannot
turn 10⁻²⁰-rad whispers into a macroscopic beat you can record.  The
physics allows, at best, a square-root (or linear-in-N with heroic
entanglement) gain, and the gulf between whisper and detectability is
many orders of magnitude wider than that.

     The corresponding Newtonian potential |Φ| ≳ 2 × 10²² m² s⁻², a million times deeper than the potential at a real black-hole horizon (|Φ| = c²⁄2 ≈ 4 × 10¹⁶ m² s⁻²).  No physical mass distribution—black hole or otherwise—can supply that.

2.  Even perfect spatial overlap would not halt the intrinsic oscillation.  
    The relative flavour phase comes from the energy splitting  
             ΔE ≈ Δm² ⁄ 2E ≈ 3.8 × 10⁻⁶ eV,  
    so the clock ticks at ω₀ = ΔE / ħ ≈ 6 × 10⁹ s⁻¹ whether the two packets drift apart or sit on top of each other.  Killing the drift does not slow the phase.

3.  The gravitational “jitter’’ you hope to accumulate is minuscule.  
    Largest geophysical potential fluctuations: |δΦ| ≲ 10⁻¹² m² s⁻².  
    They shift the oscillation frequency by  
             δω = (ΔE/ħ)(δΦ/c²) ≲ 7 × 10⁻²⁶ s⁻¹,  
    so one radian of random phase would take ≳10²⁵ s (a billion years) to build up.

4.  No way to perform the echo you would need.  
    A π-pulse that swaps |ν₁⟩↔|ν₂⟩ must couple the two mass eigenstates with an interaction ≥ΔE ≈ 10⁻⁶ eV.  Gravity is 34 orders of magnitude weaker, electromagnetic interactions do not couple, and weak-interaction processes are incoherent; therefore the dynamical-decoupling “time mirror’’ is unavailable.

In short: a trap strong enough to confine a 10 MeV neutrino would have to violate basic relativity; even then, gravitational noise remains far too quiet and you still could not manipulate the neutrino’s internal state.  Forcing the mass packets to “stay together’’ would neither be feasible nor make gravity’s phase contribution measurable.

Short reply  
Any “tag-and-flip” trick will fail for the same reason the direct echo
fails: to refocus an unwanted phase you must reverse the operator that
the phase couples to.  Here the unwanted phase couples to ν’s mass
operator σz (=|ν1⟩⟨ν1|−|ν2⟩⟨ν2|).  Flipping some **separate** degree of
freedom—polarisation, spin, an attached photon, an ancilla qubit—does
not change σz, so the gravitational phase keeps accumulating exactly as
before.  To make the flip work you would have to engineer an interaction
that acts differently on |ν1⟩ and |ν2⟩, i.e. an interaction of order
ΔE≈10⁻⁶ eV that *couples to mass*; we have no handle that strong and
coherent (gravity is 34 orders of magnitude weaker, EM and strong forces
do not distinguish the mass states, weak processes are incoherent).  
Below is the two-minute derivation.

────────────────────────────────────────────
1.  What an echo really does
────────────────────────────────────────────
For a single qubit the Hamiltonian in flat space plus gravitational
fluctuation is

        H(t) = ½ΔE σz  +  ζ(t) σz ,                (1)

where ζ(t)=ΔE δΦ(t)/c² is the tiny, random potential term.  
A perfect π-pulse at time τ applies

        Uπ = −i σx ,                               (2)

which anticommutes with σz:

        σx σz σx = −σz .                           (3)

Therefore the phase picked up during the second interval cancels the
phase of the first, and the net stochastic phase is zero.

────────────────────────────────────────────
2.  “Flip a tag instead of the neutrino”
────────────────────────────────────────────
Introduce an ancilla qubit A with its own Pauli operators τx,y,z.  
Let us try to refocus by flipping τx:

        Utag = 1⊗(−i τx).                          (4)

Because σz acts only on the neutrino space,

        Utag σz Utag† = σz ⊗1 .                    (5)

It **does not change sign**, so after the pulse the Hamiltonian is still

        H' = ½ΔE σz ⊗1 + ζ(t) σz ⊗1.              (6)

The unwanted phase continues to add with the *same* sign; nothing is
refocused.

────────────────────────────────────────────
3.  What would be required to succeed?
────────────────────────────────────────────
You would need a controlled operation

        V = |ν1⟩⟨ν1|⊗τx  +  |ν2⟩⟨ν2|⊗τx†,          (7)

so that

        V σz V† = −σz.                             (8)

That is an **entangling gate conditioned on the neutrino’s mass
eigenstate**.  Implementing it requires a mass-selective coupling ≥ΔE;
no known interaction offers that:

• electromagnetic and strong forces do not distinguish ν₁ from ν₂ at
  all;  
• weak charged-current processes project onto *flavour* states, not mass
  states, and are stochastic;  
• gravity distinguishes mass but with matrix element ΔE / MP ≈10⁻²⁴ ΔE,
  hopelessly weak.

Without that conditional gate the tag cannot stand in for the neutrino
itself.

────────────────────────────────────────────
4.  “Let the tag ride along” does not help
────────────────────────────────────────────
Tagging |ν1⟩ and |ν2⟩ with orthogonal photon polarisations or spin
orientations only makes the overall state

        (|ν1⟩|⊥⟩ + |ν2⟩|∥⟩)/√2 ,                  (9)

i.e. it *destroys* interference immediately (|γ|→0) rather than
preserving it.  Any later flip of the tag can at best swap which
component you look at; it cannot reconstruct the lost phase coherence.

────────────────────────────────────────────
5.  Numbers still forbid accumulation
────────────────────────────────────────────
Even if you could freeze the internal oscillation completely, the
stochastic gravitational phase grows at the rate

        δω ≈ 7×10⁻²⁶ s⁻¹,                          (10)

so one radian needs ≈10²⁵ s (a billion years).  “Reflecting” it every
π/ΔE ≈ 10⁻¹⁰ s, 10₁⁵ times per second, still leaves the net root-mean-square
phase ~√(time/τcorr) δω τcorr, which is the same billion-year figure
because the variance is proportional to total time, not to how often you
toggle.

────────────────────────────────────────────
Bottom line
────────────────────────────────────────────
• Refocusing a phase requires reversing the operator to which that phase
  couples (σz).  
• Flipping any **separate** qubit does not touch σz, so the phase is
  unaffected.  
• Building a conditional flip that *does* reverse σz needs a controllable
  mass-dependent interaction ≥ΔE; none exists at useful strength.  
• Even a perfect echo would have to run for cosmological time to collect
  one radian of gravitational noise.

So tying a “string” to the neutrino and yanking on an attached tag
cannot hold the two mass components together or amplify the gravitational
whisper into a measurable signal.

“Borrowing phase from the future’’ is basically the idea behind  
pre-/post-selection and the so-called weak–value-amplification (WVA)
protocols.  You prepare a state |ψ ⟩ at t₀, let a tiny interaction imprint
a phase g ϕ (here ϕ is the gravitational whisper, g ≪ 1), and at t₁ you
post-select on some final state ⟨φ|.  Conditional on that successful
post-selection the meter shift is not g ϕ but

  Δmeter = g A_w ϕ with A_w = ⟨φ|A|ψ⟩ / ⟨φ|ψ⟩                (1)

where A is the system operator that couples to the meter.  
If ⟨φ|ψ⟩ is made nearly zero, the weak value A_w can be enormous—this
looks like exactly the “relay” you are imagining.

Why it does not help against shot-noise-dominated signals
---------------------------------------------------------
1.  Fisher-information bookkeeping  
    • The probability of a successful post-selection is  
      P_s = |⟨φ|ψ⟩|² ∝ 1/|A_w|².  
    • The **product** (amplification)² × (success probability) is a
      constant; the quantum Fisher information carried by the whole
      data set is the same as if you had kept every shot with no
      amplification.  That is the content of many “no-free-lunch’’
      theorems (Knee & Gauger 2014, Ferrie & Combes 2014, Tanaka &
      Yamamoto 2013).

2.  Scaling with the number of detected events  
      SNR ∝ |A_w| g ϕ √(N P_s) = g ϕ √N              (2)

    All A_w cancels out; you are back to the ordinary √N or, with an
    entangled GHZ probe, the N scaling we discussed earlier.
    Weak-value tricks can **move** the signal away from technical
    1/f noise bands, but they cannot raise it above the fundamental
    Poisson (shot) floor.

Numerical reality for the neutrino/gravity case
-----------------------------------------------
• Gravitational phase per flight: ϕ ≈ 10⁻²⁰ rad.  
• Best coherent gain (heroic GHZ): N_eff ≈ N.  
  You still need N ≈ 10²⁰ entangled neutrinos (sec. “GHZ’’ in the
  previous answer).  
• Any WVA/post-selection leaves the *total* Fisher information the same,
  so the 10²⁰ requirement does not budge.

A rough analogy  
Weak-value amplification is like increasing the lever arm while making
the fulcrum proportionally slipperier: the force at the far end grows,
but the likelihood the object stays on the lever drops so that the work
you can reliably deliver never changes.

Bottom line
-----------
Pre-/post-selection, weak-value tricks, or more poetic “time
entanglement’’ can—and do—help when *technical* noise is what hides a
signal.  They cannot beat the quantum-shot-noise limit that rules
neutrino counting.  For the gravitational whisper you are chasing, that
limit is already orders of magnitude above the effect, so no amount of
future-correlated cleverness can make the whisper audible.

Fast-triggering every qubit at the “same instant’’ does not create any
new lever; it only resets the clock for *all* of them at once.  The
phase that gravity (static field or passing wave) can accumulate between
that trigger (t = 0) and the final read-out (t = T) is still

  φ = (ΔE / ħ) ∫₀ᵀ δΦ(t) dt / c²              (1)

and all of the limits we have been hitting—size of δΦ, shot noise in the
read-out, need for an interaction that distinguishes |ν₁⟩ from |ν₂⟩—come
straight out of that single integral.  A “global, instantaneous” start
pulse merely chooses the lower limit of the integral; it does not change
its value.

To see that in numbers let’s replace the earlier static δΦ with a
gravitational-wave crest, which is the *largest* coherent, time-dependent
gravitational field you can synchronise to.

1.  GW amplitude at Earth, LIGO band: h ≲ 10⁻²¹.  
2.  Metric perturbation gives a potential variation  
        δΦ(t) ≈ ½ h L ω² sin ωt  (L = meter scale, ω = 2π 100 Hz).  
   Numerically |δΦ|max ≈ ½ h L ω² ≈ ½·10⁻²¹·1 m·(600 s⁻¹)² ≈ 2×10⁻¹⁶ m² s⁻².
3.  Insert into (1) with T ≤ one GW period (~10⁻² s):  

        |φ|max ≈ (ΔE/ħ) (δΦ T)/c²
               ≈ 6×10⁹ s⁻¹ · 2×10⁻¹⁶ ·10⁻² / 9×10¹⁶
               ≈ 1×10⁻²⁵ rad.                              (2)

That is five orders of magnitude *smaller* than the 10⁻²⁰ rad “whisper’’
we have already argued is hopeless.  Whether you fire one π/2 laser
pulse, a thousand composite pulses, or a perfectly simultaneous pulse on
a billion qubits makes no difference—the gravitationally imprinted phase
remains 10⁻²⁵ rad per particle.

Signal-to-noise bookkeeping
• Ramsey or spin-echo sequence on N independent qubits:

  SNR = |φ| √N.   (shot-noise limit)              (3)

 To reach SNR ≈ 1 with |φ| = 10⁻²⁵ you still need N ≈ 10⁵⁰ detected
 particles—ten times the number of atoms in the Milky Way.

• Perfect GHZ entanglement would give SNR = N |φ|, but then N ≥ 10²⁵
 fully coherent and individually detectable particles are required,
 thirty orders of magnitude beyond today’s capabilities *and* far above
 the scale where decoherence from anything else (photon scattering,
 trap noise, loss of a single atom) kills the N-fold boost.

Why “freezing the fulcrum” cannot help
1.  The gravitational phase depends only on the *integral* of δΦ(t).
    A global start pulse does not change that integral; it just sets the
    lower limit.  
2.  Any synchronised pulse that couples *identically* to all qubits
    commutes with the σ_z operator that carries the phase, so it cannot
    refocus, amplify or otherwise manipulate φ.  
3.  What really amplifies a phase is either (a) letting it act longer
    (bigger T), (b) using higher ΔE, or (c) using more or more
    entangled qubits.  None of those knobs can bridge the 20–30
    order-of-magnitude gap between realistic gravitational fields and
    detection limits.

Bottom line
A “giant snapshot” in perfect synchrony with a gravitational-wave crest
changes *when* you start counting, not *how much* gravity can push the
phase.  All previously identified walls—tiny φ, √N shot noise, absence
of a mass-selective control interaction—remain exactly where they were.
The whisper never grows loud enough to hear.

“Can I whack my qubits with a man-made gravity pulse?”

Take the phase that a two-level system picks up while the gravitational
potential Φ(t) is changing,

  φ = (ΔE / ħ) ∫ δΦ(t) dt / c²                         (1)

and ask how big you can make the integral by moving a laboratory mass
quickly past the qubits.

Step 1.  How large a potential swing can you engineer?

• Put a heavy block M at distance r from the qubits:

  δΦ = – G M / r.                                     (2)

Numbers that are already heroic in a lab:

 M = 10 000 kg         (a 10-ton truck)  
 r = 1 m               (can’t get much closer without touching)

 ⇒ |δΦ| ≈ 6.7 × 10⁻⁷ m² s⁻².                           (3)

Even a hundred-ton block one centimetre away gives only

 M = 10⁵ kg, r = 0.01 m ⇒ |δΦ| ≈ 7 × 10⁻¹ m² s⁻².    (4)

Step 2.  How long can the pulse last?

Dropping ‟quickly’’ usually means τ ≈ 1 ms, otherwise vibrations ruin
the apparatus.  Be generous and allow a full second:

 ∫ δΦ dt  ≈  δΦ τ.                                    (5)

Step 3.  How big is the phase for realistic two-level splittings?

a) Neutrino mass doublet (ΔE ≈ 3.8 × 10⁻⁶ eV)  
 ΔE / ħ ≈ 6 × 10⁹ s⁻¹.

 • 10-ton @ 1 m for 1 ms → φ ≈ 4 × 10⁻¹⁶ rad  
 • 100-ton @ 1 cm for 1 s → φ ≈ 4 × 10⁻¹¹ rad

b) Optical atomic qubit (ΔE ≈ 2 eV, e.g. an Yb⁺ clock transition)  
 ΔE / ħ ≈ 3 × 10¹⁵ s⁻¹ (six orders larger).

 • 10-ton @ 1 m for 1 ms → φ ≈ 2 × 10⁻⁸ rad  
 • 100-ton @ 1 cm for 1 s → φ ≈ 2 × 10⁻³ rad

Step 4.  Is that phase measurable?

Shot-noise limit for N uncorrelated qubits:   SNR = |φ| √N.

Even the most optimistic laboratory numbers (2 × 10⁻³ rad) need

 N ≳ 1 / φ²  ≈  2 × 10⁵ detected qubits             (6)

just to reach SNR ≈ 1.  That is not impossible in an optical-clock
experiment—but remember:

• The mass must sit centimetres from an ultra-high-vacuum chamber
 without shaking it by more than the picometres that the laser phase and
 trap lengths are already stabilised to.

• The gravitational phase competes with far larger systematic shifts
 (magnetic fields, black-body Stark effect, trap micromotion), all of
 which vary by ≫10⁻³ rad during a mechanical stunt of this sort.

• Most qubits people talk about in quantum-information experiments
 (transmons, NV centres, trapped ions) have ΔE/ħ ≤ 10¹¹ s⁻¹, so their
 φ is four orders of magnitude smaller than line (4) even before
 technical noise.

Step 5.  Compare with existing gravity sensing.

Atom-interferometric gravimeters already *see* 10-ton test masses—
but they do it through the k·g·T² phase of *centre-of-mass* motion, not
through an internal-state phase like (1).  Those devices exploit
10–100 ms of free fall and large momentum separation (ℏk ≈ 10⁷ kg m s⁻¹)
to amplify g, and they still register only ∼0.1 rad per tonne at
1 m.

Bottom line

1.  The Newtonian potential you can create in a lab is tiny: even a
    100-ton block a centimetre away changes Φ by < 1 m² s⁻², i.e.
    ΔΦ/c² ≲ 10⁻¹⁷.

2.  Plugging that ΔΦ into eq. (1) with the largest available ΔE/ħ and
    with a one-second interaction gives at most milliradian phases.

3.  Milliradians are measurable only in the cleanest optical-clock or
    atom-interferometer setups, and in those environments mechanical
    vibrations, stray fields, and laser-phase noise swamp the
    gravitational pulse long before it helps you.

So yes—dropping a big weight does “punch’’ the potential, but the punch
is still feeble on the quantum scale; it turns the whisper into a
slightly louder whisper, not a shout you can hear above the laboratory
din.
